issueReference.body:

requestedChange: Fix the broken React DevTools integration in `packages/fiber/src/core/reconciler.tsx` of the pmndrs/react-three-fiber codebase, which regressed in v9 with React 19. The current code calls `injectIntoDevTools` with the old `rendererPackageName` / `rendererVersion` API, which is no longer correct after the React 19 reconciler refactor, causing DevTools to fail silently. The solution should update the `injectIntoDevTools` call to use the React 19-compatible signature, aligning with how other custom renderers expose themselves to DevTools post-React 19.

issueReference.title: RenderPipeline: Fix sync with `renderer.outputColorSpace` and `renderer.toneMapping`
issueReference.body:
**Description**

The PR fixed the automatic shader update when `renderer.outputColorSpace` or `renderer.toneMapping` changes, which can be expected by the user when using `RenderPipeline` since it happens in the default rendering.

/cc @brunosimon

requestedChange: Fix `RenderPipeline` in Three.js to automatically re-compile shaders when `renderer.outputColorSpace` or `renderer.toneMapping` is changed at runtime. The current `RenderPipeline` does not observe changes to these renderer properties, so shaders compiled before the change continue running with stale color space and tone mapping transforms, producing incorrect output. The solution should add change-detection for these two renderer properties and trigger shader invalidation or re-compilation when they are updated.

issueReference.title: HDRLoader: Large single header file lines cause internal parsing errors
issueReference.body:
### Description

When HDRLoader is used to parse a buffer that has a header line >128 bytes long, it breaks the internal fgets implementation which makes the header parsing fail, even for valid files.

Depending on the file the exact error can differ (since any line after the long one is parsed with

requestedChange: Fix `HDRLoader` in Three.js failing to parse valid HDR files that contain header lines longer than 128 bytes. The current internal `fgets` implementation double-counts chunks after the first one, causing large lines to corrupt the parser state and cascade into failures for all subsequent header lines. The solution should fix the chunk-accumulation logic in `fgets` so it correctly handles lines of arbitrary length, allowing HDR files with long header fields to be loaded.

In large AWS Organizations, security analysts need to triage findings by Organizational Unit (OU). Currently, Prowler sends findings to Security Hub in ASFF format without including OU ID or OU name.

issueReference.title: [Regression 7.4.1] Nested Uint8Array in Json fields serialized as numeric-keyed objects instead of base64 strings
issueReference.body:

issueReference.title: 🐛 Fix, avoid yield from a TaskGroup, only as an async context manager, closed in the request async exit stack
issueReference.body:
🐛 Fix, avoid yield from a TaskGroup, only as an async context manager, closed in the request async exit stack

Ref: https://peps.python.org/pep-0789/

Discovered by extrapolating insights by @graingert while discussing another comparable external use case.

requestedChange: Fix FastAPI's dependency injection system in the fastapi/fastapi codebase to prevent `yield`-based dependencies from wrapping a `TaskGroup` in a generator, and instead manage it exclusively as an async context manager closed in the request's async exit stack. The current approach of using `yield` inside a `TaskGroup` violates PEP 789, which prohibits yielding from within a task group because it can leave tasks orphaned if the generator is abandoned mid-flight. The solution should refactor affected dependency patterns to use `async with TaskGroup()` as a context manager added to the exit stack, ensuring the task group is always properly joined on request teardown.

issueReference.title: NatSpec: "overloaded" should be "overridden" in ERC20FlashMint
issueReference.body:
In `ERC20FlashMint.sol`, the NatSpec for `_flashFee` (line 70) and `_flashFeeReceiver` (line 81) says the function "can be overloaded" when it should say "can be overridden."

In Solidity, overloading means multiple functions with the same name but different parameter types. Overriding means a child contract redefining a `virtual` function. Since both `_flashFee` and `_flashFeeReceiver` are `virtual` and meant to be overridden by inheritors, the correct term is "overridden."
